title: Victoria sponge
subtitle: The foundational British cake: equal-weight sponge with jam and cream.
excerpt: Two light vanilla sponge cakes made by the all-in-one method, sandwiched with raspberry jam and whipped double cream. The essential British celebration cake. Serves eight.
sourceNotes: Victoria sponge. Named for Queen Victoria, who is documented as a regular consumer of the cake. The equal-weight sponge recipe appears in Mrs Beeton's Book of Household Management (1861, public domain) and became the standard British sandwich cake throughout the Victorian era. The all-in-one method (creaming butter and sugar, then adding all other ingredients) was codified in 20th-century British baking.

recipe.freezeNotes: Freeze the unfilled sponges. Fill and dust with icing sugar after defrosting.
recipe.makeAheadNotes: Sponges can be baked the day before and kept wrapped. Fill on the day.

content.text: A Victoria sponge lives or dies by the creaming step. Beat the butter and sugar together for a full 5 minutes until the mixture is pale and very fluffy; this aeration is what gives the cake its light, open crumb. The rest is just assembly. Use room-temperature eggs and butter; cold ingredients split the batter.

Heat the oven to 180°C. Grease two 20 cm round cake tins and line the bases. Beat {{unsalted-butter}} of soft butter and {{caster-sugar}} of sugar with a hand mixer for 5 minutes until pale and fluffy. Add {{eggs}} eggs one at a time, beating well after each. Fold in {{self-raising-flour}} of sifted flour, {{baking-powder}} of baking powder and {{vanilla-extract}} of vanilla extract. Divide equally between the tins and level. Bake for 20 to 25 minutes until risen, golden and a skewer inserted in the centre comes out clean. Cool in the tins for 10 minutes, then on a wire rack.

Spread {{jam}} of raspberry jam over the flat side of one sponge. Top with {{double-cream}} of whipped cream. Place the second sponge on top and dust with {{icing-sugar}} of icing sugar. Serve on the day.

items.symptom: Batter curdles when eggs are added
items.cause: Cold eggs or butter, or eggs added too quickly.
items.fix: Bring everything to room temperature first. Add one egg at a time and beat well. A tablespoon of flour with the last egg prevents curdling.

items.cause: Underbaked, or oven door opened during baking.
items.fix: Test with a skewer. Do not open the oven for the first 20 minutes.

items.cause: Insufficient creaming, or flour overworked after adding.
items.fix: Cream butter and sugar for a full 5 minutes. Fold in the flour gently to preserve the air you have beaten in.

content.text: The Victoria sponge is the most British of cakes and the foundation of British baking: once you understand the creaming method, every other sandwich cake follows the same principle. It appears at village fetes, birthday teas and baking competitions. The Women's Institute standard requires a light, level sponge with even layers and a dusting of caster sugar. Icing sugar is also acceptable.
